serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chicken feet mala is approximately 350 g, comprising braised chicken feet, noodles, and mala dressing, as commonly served at Singaporean hawker stalls and food delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, feet, cooked (SR Legacy) and composite estimation for mala-seasoned preparation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for mala-seasoned chicken feet with noodles exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken feet (FDC ID 171477) combined with component-level estimates for mala sauce ingredients (soy sauce, chilli oil, broad bean paste, Sichuan peppercorns) and noodle contribution. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA component data and retained where consistent. Sodium value of 480 mg/100g is consistent with high-sodium mala sauce preparations documented in USDA branded and SR Legacy entries for similar spiced braised poultry dishes. Values are flagged as estimated.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on mixed noodle dish with protein and fat components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mala-seasoned chicken feet with noodles. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed noodle dish entries in the University of Sydney GI Database: wheat noodles in mixed protein-fat dishes typically yield GI 40–55. The high protein (11.2 g/100g) and fat (10.0 g/100g) content, combined with vinegar in the mala dressing, further attenuates glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 16.8) / 100 = 7.6 (using 350 g serving, where 4.8 g/100g × 3.5 = 16.8 g carbohydrate per serving). GL reported as 7.6 is low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg/100g from mala sauce, broad bean paste (doubanjiang), and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium content (480 mg/100g; ~1680 mg per 350 g serving), which substantially exceeds the recommended 600 mg per meal threshold; if consumed, request reduced mala sauce, substitute low-sodium soy sauce, and limit portion to no more than 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.8 g/100g) but high sodium and refined noodle component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (4.8 g/100g) and estimated low GI (~45) make this dish relatively glycaemia-friendly in small portions; however, individuals with type 2 diabetes should request reduced noodle quantity to limit refined carbohydrate load and monitor sodium intake given the elevated cardiovascular risk associated with the condition.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (480 mg/100g), cholesterol (48 mg/100g), saturated fat (3.2 g/100g), and chilli oil contributing to cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish poses a high cardiovascular concern due to the combination of elevated sodium (480 mg/100g), cholesterol (48 mg/100g from chicken feet skin and cartilage), and saturated fat (3.2 g/100g) from chilli oil and chicken skin; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume only small portions infrequently and request reduced sauce and skin removal where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg/100g exceeds 400 mg/100g RA threshold; nightshade-derived chilli and chilli oil present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to sodium exceeding the 400 mg/100g RA threshold, which may worsen tissue swelling, and the presence of nightshade-derived chilli and chilli oil that may aggravate symptoms in nightshade-sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Feet Mala (麻辣鸡脚) is a spicy and numbing braised chicken feet dish popular in Singapore's hawker and food delivery scene, drawing on both Burmese mala thoke (spicy tossed salad) and Chinese Sichuan mala flavour traditions. Chicken feet are first boiled or braised until the skin and cartilage are tender and gelatinous, then tossed in a bold mala dressing made from broad bean paste (doubanjiang), soy sauce, chilli oil, Sichuan peppercorns, dried chillies, garlic, ginger, sesame oil, and vinegar. The dish is typically served cold or at room temperature as a salad or noodle salad, often accompanied by wheat noodles and crushed peanuts. The texture is soft and gelatinous from the collagen-rich chicken feet, with a fiery, numbing, savoury, and slightly sour flavour profile. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (11.2 g/100g) primarily from chicken feet collagen and connective tissue, moderate fat (10.0 g/100g) from chilli oil and chicken skin, and low carbohydrate (4.8 g/100g) from noodles and sauce. Sodium is high at 480 mg/100g, making this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The estimated glycaemic index is low (~45) due to the protein, fat, and vinegar content attenuating glucose response. Cholesterol is moderate at 48 mg/100g from chicken skin. The dish is not suitable for halal diets without verified halal-certified mala sauce and is not vegetarian or vegan. Common serving size is approximately 350 g per bowl.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for mala-seasoned chicken feet with noodles exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken feet data combined with component-level estimates for mala sauce ingredients. Input record provenance is ai_generated with confidence 0.7. Values are internally consistent and cross-checked against USDA component data but remain estimated. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for mala-seasoned chicken feet with noodles is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish preparation is not reported in available approved sources. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific mala-seasoned chicken feet preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 4. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed noodle dishes with protein and fat components from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 5. Input record had two portions both marked is_default: true (450 g and 250 g); serving size resolved to 350 g as a midpoint representative of a standard hawker bowl. 6. Halal status is unconfirmed as mala sauce and broad bean paste sourcing requires halal certification verification. 7. trans_fat_g value of 0.04 g/100g is retained from input; this trace level is consistent with minor trans fat from chilli oil and is not confirmed zero — value is plausible and within detection range.
